nativeRange.habitat: Wetlands, marshes, and shallow water margins of streams and ponds across East Asia; grows both submersed and emersed in nature

decorPreferences: nutrient-rich substrate or fine gravel preferred for anchoring stems
midground to background planting area, best in groups of stems
tolerates open swimming space above the planting

goodWith: fry that benefit from shelter in trailing foliage
avoidWith: plant-eating fish such as goldfish, silver dollars, or large cichlids
warm-water-only setups sustained above ~27 degrees C

summary: Cardamine lyrata (Japanese Cress) is an easy stem plant from East Asian wetlands with delicate, trailing clover-like foliage. It is unusually cold-tolerant, grows both submersed and emersed, and produces aerial roots along its stems.
careNotes: Cardamine lyrata is a hardy, beginner-friendly stem plant native to wetlands across Japan, China, and Korea. It is one of relatively few aquarium stems that is genuinely cold-tolerant — generally advised to keep below ~26-28 degrees C, as sustained warmer water tends to produce smaller leaves and leggier growth. The species is described in sources as suitable for unheated tanks and even outdoor garden ponds during summer. Lighting demand is reported variably across sources: Tropica describes it as low-demand, while Buce Plant, Fish Laboratory, and Aqua-Fish describe it as medium-to-bright; a medium-light setup is a reasonable middle-ground that maintains compact growth without forcing legginess. CO2 is not strictly required (Tropica reports a medium CO2 demand of 6-14 mg/L, while Fish Laboratory describes it as optional but beneficial); injection improves growth rate and density. Substrate is flexible — fine gravel or nutrient-rich aquasoil both work; cuttings root readily from the stem. Both submerged and emersed forms are common, and aerial roots tend to form along the stems. Cross-source disagreement notes: temperature ceilings range from 24 degrees C (Aqua-Fish) to 28 degrees C (Tropica/Fish Laboratory) — recorded as a 15-26 degrees C window reflecting the cold-tolerant character; size range varies from 12.5-25 cm (Buce Plant) to 20-50 cm (Tropica/Fish Laboratory) — recorded as 20-50 cm to reflect submerged growth potential when not trimmed. Minimum tank volume of 40 L is an estimated guideline for a small group of stems; sources did not state a specific volume.
breedingNotes: Propagation is straightforward via stem cuttings: trim a healthy lateral shoot or top section and replant directly into the substrate, where it readily roots. Dense clumps can also be divided by splitting the rootstock. Aerial roots developing along the stems indicate good vigor and can themselves be used as propagation points.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Primary source. Tropica describes Cardamine lyrata as an easy marsh plant from Japan with low light demand, medium CO2 (6-14 mg/L), fast growth, height 20-30+ cm, characteristic trailing stems with aerial roots, and suitability for unheated tanks and summer garden ponds. Note: the manifest's pre-supplied URL (4423/4423) actually serves Microsorum pteropus 'Windeløv'; corrected to 4441/4441 via Tropica's own search to reach the actual Cardamine lyrata page.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked common names (Japanese Cress, Chinese Ivy), family (Brassicaceae), Asian origin, pH 6.5-7.5, medium lighting, CO2 recommended, moderate-to-fast growth, midground placement, and stem-cutting propagation.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature range (60-82 F = 15.5-27.8 C), pH 5-8, medium-to-strong lighting, optional CO2, fast growth, midground/background placement, stem-cutting propagation, max size ~50 cm, and easy difficulty.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net
additional.notes: Used for full native range (China, Japan, Korea), water hardness range (4-18 dGH), temperature 15-24 C, pH 6-8, midground-to-background placement with paludarium suitability, and emersed-vs-submerged growth confirmation.
